danger_signals.visual_form: numerical position limit displayed on HUD that counts down during race
danger_signals.meaning: player must maintain minimum placement or face game over; falling below threshold indicates imminent failure state
time pressure signal; failure to complete lap before timer ends results in immediate game over

danger_signals.visual_form: top 20% of screen showing vehicles approaching from behind
danger_signals.meaning: incoming collision hazard; opponent vehicles closing distance signal danger of being overtaken or hit

danger_signals.visual_form: visual environmental change (wet track appearance after qualifying in top 3)
danger_signals.meaning: difficulty escalation; progression to harder race conditions signals increased challenge/danger

safety_signals.meaning: player has secured entry to main race and avoided immediate failure; safe progression to next phase

safety_signals.visual_form: finishing race in third place or better
safety_signals.meaning: achievement of safe performance threshold; allows advancement to next challenge level

safety_signals.visual_form: notification/UI element indicating invitation to better racing team
safety_signals.meaning: progression signal; player has performed well enough to earn faster car, indicating safe advancement in world championship

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: computer-controlled opponent drivers with distinct liveries representing different teams
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: team identity conveyed through car visual design and racing team hierarchy; player can be invited to join better teams during season

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: 19 competing drivers in arcade mode; multiple opponents in championship mode representing different national teams
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: multiple real-world race circuits (Brands Hatch, Hockenheimring, Monaco) serve as faction territory markers representing 1989 F1 World Championship locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: practice lap display with 45-second countdown timer; player positioned alone on track
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player must complete single lap within time limit; computer opponents not yet active; failure ends game immediately

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 19 computer-controlled drivers visible on track; position counter displayed showing current grid placement and minimum threshold
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player must maintain above position limit throughout three laps; opponents actively racing and competing for positions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: environmental visual change indicating rain/wet track surface; activated after qualifying in top 3
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: increased difficulty; handling becomes more challenging; visual manifestation of world state progression to harder difficulty tier

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: multiple circuit locations from 1989 F1 calendar displayed; team upgrade notifications appear
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player races across different tracks; team invitations trigger car upgrades; password system enables long-form progression tracking

environmental_storytelling.example: Monaco Grand Prix circuit (albeit fictionalized with modifications) establishes racing prestige context; real 1989 F1 circuits in Genesis Championship mode (Brands Hatch, Hockenheimring) ground game in authentic motorsport calendar
top 20% of screen dedicated to rear-view mirror communicates player vulnerability from behind; spatial storytelling element showing player perspective and imminent threats
player choice between automatic, 4-speed, and 7-speed manual transmission before races communicates vehicle complexity and racing team professionalism; choice carries gameplay consequence

World Championship mode allows player to be 'invited to join a better racing team' with faster car; this communicates upward progression through team hierarchy without explicit narrative, purely through mechanical reward

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: must complete lap within 45-second window; performance determines starting grid position; isolated from opponent interaction
must maintain position above countdown limit; interactions with 19 computer opponents; position changes reflected in real-time counter
dynamically position themselves on track; visible in rear-view mirror when approaching; represent threat to player position maintenance
player races in initial team; visible through car visual design and performance characteristics
after invitation, player receives faster car; visual and performance change signals world state progression and earned advancement

notes: Super Monaco GP communicates world state primarily through mechanical systems (timers, position counters) rather than narrative elements. The game uses spatial design (rear-view mirror) and visual progression (wet conditions unlock, team upgrades) to manifest danger and safety states. Faction identity is implicit through racing team hierarchy rather than explicit visual markers like colors or symbols. The Genesis version adds world state complexity through multi-circuit championship mode, creating progression through geography and team advancement. No explicit political allegiances are communicated beyond Formula One championship structure.
